top_strategic.title: Taxonomy Franchise: 'Brand Rules Engine for [Sport]'
top_strategic.evidence_summary: License our methodology (not just rules, but the *decision loop itself*) to tennis, padel, and squash data companies. They upload their corpus; we train a sport-specific classifier and charge them per classification + monthly seats. We become the infrastructure layer for sports brand intelligence.

counterargument.argument: Licensing the decision loop itself assumes your pickleball rules are generalizable to tennis/padel/squash, which have completely different equipment, court layouts, and sponsorship norms.
counterargument.risk: You'll export rules that don't transfer (e.g., 'paddle logo rules' don't apply to tennis rackets), creating broken classifiers in partner systems that reflect back on your credibility. Silent failures in downstream partner dashboards will be impossible to debug without access to their data.

top_strategic.title: Brand Licensing Network (Marketplace Play)
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Build a two-sided marketplace: brands can register themselves + their *approved appearance rules* (e.g., 'JOOLA logos only on paddles, not on apparel'). Venues/streamers query the API: 'Is this appearance compliant?' Revenue from brands (verification), venues (lookups), and licensees (premium terms). You become brand-athlete-venue arbiter.

counterargument.argument: A brand-managed marketplace assumes brands will self-report accurate appearance rules, which creates garbage-in-garbage-out risk and shifts taxonomy maintenance burden to external actors.
counterargument.risk: Brands will either over-report (claiming restrictions that don't exist) or under-report (hiding restrictions) to game visibility. Your classifier will now be a patchwork of self-served rules you can't audit. Downstream consumers (venues, reports) will lose trust when rules contradict each other or change without notice.

top_strategic.title: Brand Safety API for Sports Broadcasters
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Sell real-time classification-as-a-service to ESPN, YouTube Gaming, and Twitch so they can auto-flag, blur, or report unlicensed brand appearances in live sports. We own the taxonomy + rules; they own the distribution. Revenue: per-match SaaS + data licensing.

counterargument.argument: Real-time blur/flag API on live broadcasts creates legal liability if your classifier misidentifies a brand or flags a legitimate appearance, especially if broadcasters auto-act on your output without human review.
counterargument.risk: A single false positive that causes ESPN to blur a sponsor's logo during a live match is a lawsuit and reputation damage. You're shifting classification risk from internal rules to external action—broadcasters will demand SLAs and indemnification you can't provide. Silent classifier drift will now have immediate on-air consequences.

top_strategic.title: Brand Intel Consortium (Cooperative Play)
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Partner with 5–10 sports data companies (tennis, padel, esports, etc.) to pool decision logs and taxonomies into a shared neutral registry. Sell *aggregate cross-sport brand trends* back to CPG companies and agencies ('Where is Nike gaining mindshare across racquet sports?'). You own the data backbone and licensing terms.

counterargument.argument: A neutral registry across 5–10 companies assumes you can align taxonomies and governance models—both are expensive migrations that typically take 12+ months and fail.
counterargument.risk: Each partner has different classification needs and political incentives. Merging taxonomies will either water down precision (by averaging to lowest-common-denominator rules) or create silos (defeating the 'pooling' goal). You'll spend more time on governance than on the actual product signal.

top_strategic.title: Competitor Intelligence Subscription (Dark Pattern Play)
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Run the classifier on all public/competitor footage (tournaments, YouTube, TikTok) and sell monthly reports: 'What brands are gaining shelf-space in pickleball? Where is your brand losing visibility?' Monetize FOMO and market share anxiety. Data moat: only we have the calibrated rules.

counterargument.argument: Monitoring competitor footage without explicit rights may expose you to copyright and right-of-publicity claims, especially if you're re-selling competitor intelligence.
counterargument.risk: This is a legal minefield disguised as a product finding. Even if footage is public, scraping and monetizing it without consent invites cease-and-desist letters from tournament operators and athletes. The 0.82 confidence is borrowed credibility—this is a business/legal decision, not a classifier finding.

top_strategic.title: Automated Rights-Management Workflow Tool
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Sell to venues and tournament organizers: upload match footage, our classifier auto-flags every brand appearance, auto-generates licensing invoices, and auto-rejects illegal appearances in real-time broadcasts. Revenue: per-venue + per-match micro-transactions. Becomes essential ops infra.

counterargument.argument: Auto-invoicing based on classifier output creates financial liability—venues will dispute invoices if your classifier misses a brand or false-positives a competitor's logo.
counterargument.risk: You're automating a legal/financial workflow on top of a system with 7.4% fallback rate and unknown precision on edge cases. Venues will demand audit trails and appeal mechanisms, turning your classifier into a payment processor with compliance overhead. One major invoice dispute will kill the product.

top_data.title: Top 10 unknown brands account for 314 detections — high-leverage rule targets
top_data.evidence_summary: Most frequent unknowns: JOOLA(220): 'shirt chest'; 7(21): 'Signage on the fence in the background'; R&M(16): 'Top left corner of the video feed overla'; goomba(16): 'Top left corner of the video feed overla'; Unknown(9): 'worn by the primary player'; UG(9): 'Banner on the wall behind the court'. A rule for any of these immediately converts N detections from unknown to classified. Sort by frequency and research top 5.
top_data.recommended_action: For each top unknown: 1-call Haiku research ('what is X in the pickleball world?'); propose rule.

location_samples.7: Signage on the fence in the background
location_samples.R&M: Top left corner of the video feed overlay
location_samples.goomba: Top left corner of the video feed overlay
location_samples.UG: Banner on the wall behind the court
location_samples.R&M_fox_wifi_goomba: top left corner of the video feed overlay
location_samples.R8M: Top left corner of the video feed overlay
location_samples.2026-01-05: Top left corner of the video feed overlay
location_samples.Foz_4_UG_Court: Signage on the wall behind the court.

counterargument.argument: JOOLA 'shirt chest' detections and fence signage are fundamentally different classification problems—one is legitimate brand presence, the other is background clutter.
counterargument.risk: Treating all 314 detections as 'rule gaps' will cause you to add rules that capture noise (background signage, overlays) alongside genuine brand appearances, degrading precision. You'll need to manually audit each cluster first—research calls alone won't tell you whether a pattern should become a rule or a filter.

top_data.title: 7.4% of classifications are fallback (no rule match) — every % is a rule gap
top_data.evidence_summary: 340 detections use fallback classification vs 4261 that match a rule (92.6% rule coverage). Fallback is the classifier guessing — each one is a rule we haven't written yet.
top_data.recommended_action: Cluster fallback detections and promote the top clusters into new rules.

counterargument.argument: 7.4% fallback rate is low enough that clustering and promoting top clusters may introduce false positives that break existing high-precision rules.
counterargument.risk: You'll be adding rules based on only 340 samples across potentially dozens of clusters—each with <50 examples. New rules built on thin evidence will trigger false positives on edge cases, polluting downstream reports and forcing you to maintain exception lists. The classifier owner likely already knows these gaps exist and chose not to fill them for precision reasons.

top_data.title: 1 rules look right but you override them often
top_data.evidence_summary: DUDE CLOTHING: 1/2 decisions overridden (4 corpus fires). Sample note: "Probably something there. Keep looking out for underserved brands and try to build some confidence around it. It could be fun and rewarding." These rules pass the auto-verified bar but your manual review keeps correcting them — the classification they produce is technically legal but rarely what you actually want.
top_data.recommended_action: Adjust the rule defaults to match the override you keep making.

Oh, okay, this is cool. You actually have some versions of uploaded videos that I put in for manual AI analysis. Let's definitely classify these as much as possible, and there should be a lot more dat

top_data.title: 30 items in investigate queue read like real brands
top_data.evidence_summary: Likely real brands stuck waiting: PUSHIT; PKLR; PBS; PSA; PKLR; PBS. Their rationales mention product context (paddle, apparel, sponsor, etc.). If left unclassified, they inflate the unknown bucket and hide signal.
top_data.recommended_action: Auto-research each via web lookup, draft a proposed rule, ship for review.

top_data.title: 15 decisions override the classifier's proposed category — override-to-decision ratio = 20.3%
top_data.evidence_summary: Top override categories: {'broadcast_tech': 3, 'unknown': 2, 'court_surface': 2}. Top override values: {'medium': 9, 'high': 3, 'low': 2}. Every override is a signal the rule's default is slightly off. High override-to-decision ratio = classifier is running behind the taxonomy.
top_data.recommended_action: For override categories you repeat, update the rule's default — don't make yourself say it twice.

top_data.title: Your 'investigate' decisions cluster into 5 reasons — the classifier could route future ones automatically
top_data.evidence_summary: Clustering 30 investigate-notes via Haiku: needs visual evidence to validate (e.g. BHP); ambiguous classification type (e.g. ODESSA); signage vs apparel distinction needed (e.g. PKLR); future value with better data infrastructure (e.g. R&M_foz_wifi_goomba); promising but requires deeper investigation (e.g. DUDE CLOTHING). Each theme is a rule the classifier could apply itself: when a new brand matches theme X, auto-route to investigate with that rationale pre-filled.
top_data.recommended_action: Add a 'pre-route' step: before surfacing unknowns for your review, apply these themes and pre-fill the rationale + override_category.

themes.rationale: PO cannot make a decision without seeing pictures or video clips of the brand appearance in context
Could be a brand, location, or other entity type - needs context to disambiguate what it actually is
Need to clarify whether brand appears as worn apparel, signage, banners, or watermarks before classifying

themes.theme: future value with better data infrastructure
themes.rationale: Could become valuable if system is built to tie classifications to specific cameras or other contextual metadata
Interesting prospect that warrants further analysis before final approval decision

top_data.title: 8 probable taxonomy merges would consolidate 911 items
top_data.evidence_summary: Candidate merges: headwear(1) + hat(1) → headwear (2 items); shoe_brand(1) + shoes(409) → shoes (410 items); clothing_brand(9) + apparel(40) + apparel_top(22) + apparel_bottom(2) + apparel_graphic(1) → apparel (74 items); drink_bottle(3) + water_bottle(1) → water_bottle (4 items); camera_equipment(1) + camera(45) → camera (46 items); watermark(1) + broadcast_tech(14) → broadcast_tech (15 items). These categories likely refer to the same concept under different names. Keeping them split fragments category analytics and confuses downstream consumers.
top_data.recommended_action: Pick one canonical name per merge group, run a migration on classified-corpus.json, bump taxonomy_version.

top_data.title: 5 brands are classified as multiple primary categories across clips
top_data.evidence_summary: Examples: PUSHIT: court_surface×5 + sponsor_signage×4 (of 10); DUNLOP: sponsor_signage×8 + ball×2 (of 10); Franklin: paddle×7 + ball×2 (of 9); PKLR: clothing_brand×6 + apparel_top×2 (of 9); Under Armour: apparel×4 + shoes×2 (of 6). When the same canonical_name gets different category_primary values on different clips, downstream aggregation double-counts or under-counts. The rule is either too loose or the brand legitimately sells in multiple categories.
top_data.recommended_action: For each: decide if the split is real (brand sells in both) or a rule bug. If real, add secondary-category convention; if bug, tighten the when_category clauses.

top_data.title: Haiku rescues 1 real brands from the top-15 unknown bucket
top_data.evidence_summary: Claude Haiku verdicts: {'real_brand': 1, 'noise': 7, 'new_category': 4}. Proposed as real brands: JOOLA. Each gets a recommended category — review and convert to rules.
top_data.recommended_action: Accept the real_brand verdicts, draft proposed-rules entries, ship for morning review.

verdicts.reasoning: JOOLA is a legitimate and major pickleball paddle/equipment manufacturer with significant market presence.
A single digit on fence signage is likely a court number or marking, not a brand.
Appears consistently in video overlay corner, likely a broadcast/production identifier or watermark.
Consistently appears in overlay corner alongside other identifiers, likely a production/streaming platform or tool name.
Placeholder text indicating failed brand recognition or OCR, not an actual brand.
Appears on court signage/banners; likely a venue, organization, or court identifier rather than a consumer brand.
Concatenated string combining overlay elements and Wi-Fi SSID fragments, clear OCR/parsing artifact.
Appears in overlay corner as variant of R&M; likely OCR corruption or duplicate detection of broadcast element.
Date string in overlay is metadata/timestamp, not a brand.
Concatenated court identifier and signage elements, appears to be parsing artifact from multiple text sources.
Date string in overlay is metadata/timestamp, not a brand.
Capitalized variant of goomba appearing in overlay corner; likely broadcast/production platform identifier.

top_data.title: 2 approved rules fire almost never — cleanup candidates
top_data.evidence_summary: Of 49 approved rules, 2 fire ≤1 time against the corpus. 2 of those are flagged as revenue-relevant (not 'none'). Examples: CRBN, SELKIRK. Either the brand is absent from the corpus or the rule's aliases miss the actual spellings.
top_data.recommended_action: Review each dead rule — tighten aliases, merge with a sibling, or delete.

all_findings.recommended_action: Review each dead rule — tighten aliases, merge with a sibling, or delete.

all_findings.title: Top 5 rules carry 3861 of 4261 classifier fires
all_findings.evidence_summary: Stars: JOOLA (3295 fires), NIKE (319 fires), PICKLEBALL (116 fires), HEAD (74 fires), ADIDAS (57 fires). These rules fire often AND almost never get overridden — they're doing the heavy lifting. Corpus has 4601 total detections, 92.6% coverage.
all_findings.recommended_action: Don't touch these. Use them as templates for new brand rules.

all_findings.evidence_summary: Candidate merges: headwear(1) + hat(1) → headwear (2 items); shoe_brand(1) + shoes(409) → shoes (410 items); clothing_brand(9) + apparel(40) + apparel_top(22) + apparel_bottom(2) + apparel_graphic(1) → apparel (74 items); drink_bottle(3) + water_bottle(1) → water_bottle (4 items); camera_equipment(1) + camera(45) → camera (46 items); watermark(1) + broadcast_tech(14) → broadcast_tech (15 items). These categories likely refer to the same concept under different names. Keeping them split fragments category analytics and confuses downstream consumers.

all_findings.title: 13 taxonomy categories have ≤3 members — noise or genuine rarity?
all_findings.evidence_summary: Singleton-ish categories: hat(1), apparel_graphic(1), headwear(1), camera_equipment(1), watermark(1), water_bottle(1), shoe_brand(1), tech_accessory(1), other(2), apparel_bottom(2). A new category should only exist if it will sustain ≥5 detections. Below that, it's either a mistype (same concept as another category) or too rare to sell against.
all_findings.recommended_action: For each singleton: delete, merge into a bigger category, or mark 'archive'.

all_findings.evidence_summary: Examples: PUSHIT: court_surface×5 + sponsor_signage×4 (of 10); DUNLOP: sponsor_signage×8 + ball×2 (of 10); Franklin: paddle×7 + ball×2 (of 9); PKLR: clothing_brand×6 + apparel_top×2 (of 9); Under Armour: apparel×4 + shoes×2 (of 6). The rule is either too loose or the brand legitimately sells in multiple categories.
all_findings.recommended_action: For each: decide if the split is real (brand sells in both) or a rule bug. If real, add secondary-category convention; if bug, tighten the when_category clauses.

all_findings.title: Unknown bucket holds 319 items (6.9% of all classifications)
all_findings.evidence_summary: The 'unknown' primary category has 319 items out of 4601 total — 6.9%. Every unknown is either a brand we haven't written a rule for yet or noise (OCR artifacts, Wi-Fi SSIDs, single letters on fences). The Unknown Surfer researcher digs into WHICH.
all_findings.recommended_action: Run Unknown Surfer; auto-research top clusters; convert winners to rules.

all_findings.title: You write decisions at 3 speeds: 33 approve, 38 investigate, 3 reject
all_findings.evidence_summary: Across 74 total decisions: {'approve': 33, 'investigate': 38, 'reject': 3}. Revenue tags: {'none': 21, 'medium': 21, 'high': 10, 'low': 22}. 48/74 decisions carry a free-text note (avg 192 chars). The notes are where the policy lives — any future predictor has to read them, not just the action.
all_findings.recommended_action: Use this distribution as the prior — a predictor that guesses 'approve' every time scores the base rate.

all_findings.title: 5 brands changed decision across review cycles — you're second-guessing yourself
all_findings.evidence_summary: Examples: PUSHIT: investigate → approve; Foz Bullet UG 4K: investigate → approve; PBS: investigate → investigate → approve; BHP: investigate → approve; DUDE CLOTHING: investigate → approve. When the same brand flips approve/investigate/reject across reviews, the decision isn't stable — either the evidence changed, or the policy is still forming. These brands deserve a note on WHY the flip.
all_findings.recommended_action: Surface these on the morning report with both rationales so you can reconcile.

Silent classifier drift will now have immediate on-air consequences.

all_findings.title: Brand Intel Consortium (Cooperative Play)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Partner with 5–10 sports data companies (tennis, padel, esports, etc.) to pool decision logs and taxonomies into a shared neutral registry. Sell *aggregate cross-sport brand trends* back to CPG companies and agencies ('Where is Nike gaining mindshare across racquet sports?'). You own the data backbone and licensing terms.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: partnership_flip play

counterargument.argument: A neutral registry across 5–10 companies assumes you can align taxonomies and governance models—both are expensive migrations that typically take 12+ months and fail.
counterargument.risk: Each partner has different classification needs and political incentives. Merging taxonomies will either water down precision (by averaging to lowest-common-denominator rules) or create silos (defeating the 'pooling' goal). You'll spend more time on governance than on the actual product signal.

all_findings.title: Competitor Intelligence Subscription (Dark Pattern Play)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Run the classifier on all public/competitor footage (tournaments, YouTube, TikTok) and sell monthly reports: 'What brands are gaining shelf-space in pickleball? Where is your brand losing visibility?' Monetize FOMO and market share anxiety. Data moat: only we have the calibrated rules.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: media_play play

counterargument.argument: Monitoring competitor footage without explicit rights may expose you to copyright and right-of-publicity claims, especially if you're re-selling competitor intelligence.
counterargument.risk: This is a legal minefield disguised as a product finding. Even if footage is public, scraping and monetizing it without consent invites cease-and-desist letters from tournament operators and athletes. The 0.82 confidence is borrowed credibility—this is a business/legal decision, not a classifier finding.

all_findings.title: Automated Rights-Management Workflow Tool
all_findings.evidence_summary: Sell to venues and tournament organizers: upload match footage, our classifier auto-flags every brand appearance, auto-generates licensing invoices, and auto-rejects illegal appearances in real-time broadcasts. Revenue: per-venue + per-match micro-transactions. Becomes essential ops infra.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: tool_spinoff play

counterargument.argument: Auto-invoicing based on classifier output creates financial liability—venues will dispute invoices if your classifier misses a brand or false-positives a competitor's logo.
counterargument.risk: You're automating a legal/financial workflow on top of a system with 7.4% fallback rate and unknown precision on edge cases. Venues will demand audit trails and appeal mechanisms, turning your classifier into a payment processor with compliance overhead. One major invoice dispute will kill the product.

all_findings.title: Open-Source Classifier + Managed SaaS Upsell
all_findings.evidence_summary: Release the 49 rules and taxonomy as open-source (build community goodwill, become the standard). Monetize via: (1) managed cloud API for scale users, (2) premium rule updates ($500/mo), (3) custom taxonomy training ($10k). GitOps + trust = defensible SaaS moat.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: tool_spinoff play

all_findings.title: Creator Monetization Layer (Trust Play)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Allow pickleball content creators to run the classifier on their own footage and auto-generate brand-attribution reports for sponsorship negotiation: 'My last 10 videos featured your logo 47 times.' Creators pay per report; brands buy lead lists. You become the trusted intermediary proving creator value.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: trust_play play

counterarguments.title: Licensing the decision loop itself assumes your pickleball rules are generalizable to tennis/padel/squash, which have completely different equipment, court layouts, and sponsorship norms.
counterarguments.risk: You'll export rules that don't transfer (e.g., 'paddle logo rules' don't apply to tennis rackets), creating broken classifiers in partner systems that reflect back on your credibility. Silent failures in downstream partner dashboards will be impossible to debug without access to their data.

counterarguments.title: 7.4% fallback rate is low enough that clustering and promoting top clusters may introduce false positives that break existing high-precision rules.
counterarguments.target_title: 7.4% of classifications are fallback (no rule match) — every % is a rule gap
counterarguments.risk: You'll be adding rules based on only 340 samples across potentially dozens of clusters—each with <50 examples. New rules built on thin evidence will trigger false positives on edge cases, polluting downstream reports and forcing you to maintain exception lists. The classifier owner likely already knows these gaps exist and chose not to fill them for precision reasons.

counterarguments.title: JOOLA 'shirt chest' detections and fence signage are fundamentally different classification problems—one is legitimate brand presence, the other is background clutter.
counterarguments.target_title: Top 10 unknown brands account for 314 detections — high-leverage rule targets
counterarguments.risk: Treating all 314 detections as 'rule gaps' will cause you to add rules that capture noise (background signage, overlays) alongside genuine brand appearances, degrading precision. You'll need to manually audit each cluster first—research calls alone won't tell you whether a pattern should become a rule or a filter.

counterarguments.title: Real-time blur/flag API on live broadcasts creates legal liability if your classifier misidentifies a brand or flags a legitimate appearance, especially if broadcasters auto-act on your output without human review.
counterarguments.target_title: Brand Safety API for Sports Broadcasters
counterarguments.risk: A single false positive that causes ESPN to blur a sponsor's logo during a live match is a lawsuit and reputation damage. You're shifting classification risk from internal rules to external action—broadcasters will demand SLAs and indemnification you can't provide. Silent classifier drift will now have immediate on-air consequences.

counterarguments.title: Monitoring competitor footage without explicit rights may expose you to copyright and right-of-publicity claims, especially if you're re-selling competitor intelligence.
counterarguments.risk: This is a legal minefield disguised as a product finding. Even if footage is public, scraping and monetizing it without consent invites cease-and-desist letters from tournament operators and athletes. The 0.82 confidence is borrowed credibility—this is a business/legal decision, not a classifier finding.

counterarguments.title: A brand-managed marketplace assumes brands will self-report accurate appearance rules, which creates garbage-in-garbage-out risk and shifts taxonomy maintenance burden to external actors.
counterarguments.risk: Brands will either over-report (claiming restrictions that don't exist) or under-report (hiding restrictions) to game visibility. Your classifier will now be a patchwork of self-served rules you can't audit. Downstream consumers (venues, reports) will lose trust when rules contradict each other or change without notice.

counterarguments.title: A neutral registry across 5–10 companies assumes you can align taxonomies and governance models—both are expensive migrations that typically take 12+ months and fail.
counterarguments.risk: Each partner has different classification needs and political incentives. Merging taxonomies will either water down precision (by averaging to lowest-common-denominator rules) or create silos (defeating the 'pooling' goal). You'll spend more time on governance than on the actual product signal.

counterarguments.title: Auto-invoicing based on classifier output creates financial liability—venues will dispute invoices if your classifier misses a brand or false-positives a competitor's logo.
counterarguments.risk: You're automating a legal/financial workflow on top of a system with 7.4% fallback rate and unknown precision on edge cases. Venues will demand audit trails and appeal mechanisms, turning your classifier into a payment processor with compliance overhead. One major invoice dispute will kill the product.
